---
bibliography: refs.bib
---
## Principal findings
This study constitutes the first systematic cetacean survey conducted within the BGTW. Across [n] survey days, [n] on-effort sightings of [n] species were recorded, dominated by [species]. Encounter rate, expressed as sightings per unit effort, varied significantly among observation stations (Kruskal–Wallis: χ² = 14.13, df = 2, p = 0.001), being highest at Europa Point, and the kernel density surface identified the southern tip of the peninsula as the principal area of elevated relative density. Detection-corrected density for the surveyed sectors was estimated at [D̂] individuals km⁻² ([95% CI]). Contrary to previously reported patterns [@olaya-ponzoneSpatioTemporalPatternsLimited2026], encounter rate was higher on the eastern than the western side of the study area, identifying a previously undersampled sector. Beyond these results, the study provides a transferable, low-cost framework for systematic monitoring in geographically bounded territorial waters and establishes a baseline against which future spatial and temporal change can be assessed.


## Spatial heterogeneity
Cetacean occurrence within the surveyed sectors of the BGTW was strongly spatially structured, concentrating at the southern tip of the peninsula (Europa Point) rather than distributing evenly across the territory. Both the effort-standardised encounter rate (@fig-SPUE) and the group-size-weighted kernel density surface (@fig-kde_map) identified this southern concentration, and, critically, the detection-corrected density estimate preserved the same ranking, with Europa Point exceeding the other sectors by more than an order of magnitude across all tested truncation distances (@tbl-sens). This concentration is consistent with the findings of De Stephanis et al. (2008), who linked cetacean distribution in the adjacent Strait of Gibraltar to oceanographic and bathymetric structure rather than to horizontal position, and with the canyon-associated aggregations reported by Olaya-Ponzone et al. (2025a) in the Bay of Algeciras.

The most plausible driver is enhanced local productivity: currents around Europa Point are stronger than at the other locations, promoting upwelling and elevating primary production, and thereby creating favourable foraging conditions [@sanchez-garridoAlboranSeaCirculation2022] (@app-bath). A secondary, non-exclusive explanation is reduced disturbance; the southern sector experiences less commercial bunkering than the Bay, and vessel presence is known to alter cetacean behaviour and habitat use in this region [@olaya-ponzoneVesselInducedBehavioural2025], though this cannot be tested with the present data and is offered tentatively. A detectability artefact is unlikely to account for the pattern; although Europa was surveyed from the lowest platform (18 m a.s.l.), which compresses the effective detection range, the persistence of the Europa maximum in the detection-corrected estimates (@tbl-sens) indicates the concentration is not solely an artefact of differing observation conditions. If confirmed across seasons, this localised concentration within an IMMA adjacent to a major traffic separation scheme implies that spatially targeted management, rather than a uniform BGTW-wide measure, is the appropriate conservation instrument.

## East–west contrast
The eastern–western asymmetry departs from previous work. Olaya-Ponzone et al. [-@olaya-ponzoneSpatioTemporalPatternsLimited2026; -@olaya-ponzoneMixedspeciesDolphinGroups2025] reported the highest detection rates within the Bay of Gibraltar and around the submarine canyon on the western side. In the present study, by contrast, per-unit-effort encounter rate was higher on the eastern side. One explanation is observational: the western vantage overlooks a bay heavily used for bunkering and anchorage, where anchored commercial vessels obstruct line of sights and the southerly location of the viewpoint limits coverage of the northern bay, reducing the reliability of species confirmation and group-size estimation. A second, non-exclusive explanation is ecological: the eastern sector is characterised by shallower bathymetry than the steeper western side [@sammartinoNumericalModelAnalysis2014; @azzellinoCetaceanResponseEnvironmental2017] and, excepting the deep-water anchorage, experiences lower vessel traffic, so it may constitute a distinct, lower-disturbance habitat. Because the eastern sector has not previously been surveyed systematically, these explanations cannot yet be separated; the finding identifies it as a priority for targeted, repeated survey effort to establish whether the pattern is consistent across seasons and years.


## Species composition and density in regional context
Three cetacean taxa were recorded during systematic survey: short-beaked common dolphins (*Delphinus delphis*, n = [n]), fin whales (*Balaenoptera physalus*, n = [n]), and dolphins that could not be identified to species level (unidentified delphinids, n = [n]). Common dolphins were the most frequently encountered taxon, consistent with their reported status as a dominant species in the wider Alborán Sea and Strait of Gibraltar [@canales-caceresDistributionBehaviourStriped2023a; @gnoneCetaceansMediterraneanSea2023; @olaya-ponzoneMixedspeciesDolphinGroups2025]. The unidentified delphinid records reflect the practical constraints of shore-based observation, where range, sea state, and brief surfacing intervals frequently precluded confident species assignment; some proportion of these are likely to represent common dolphins, with striped dolphin (*Stenella coeruleoalba*) and bottlenose dolphin (*Tursiops truncatus*) also plausible given their documented occurrence in the region [@olaya-ponzoneMixedspeciesDolphinGroups2025].

Because no systematic survey has previously been conducted within the BGTW, the density estimates reported here (@tbl-pool_dens) cannot be compared against an earlier local baseline; existing knowledge derives exclusively from opportunistic and ferry-based records that lack the effort information required for density estimation. The present estimates therefore constitute the first effort-corrected density values for the territory rather than a point of comparison with prior work, and should be interpreted as a provisional baseline. Their absolute magnitude should be treated with caution given the sample-size and design constraints discussed below (**Section [X]**); their principal value lies in establishing a reference against which future systematic surveys can assess change.

## Systematic vs. opportunistic sampling
The comparison with opportunistic records demonstrates the spatial bias inherent to platform-of-opportunity data. OBIS records of *Delphinus delphis* were strongly concentrated in the western bay, with only four records in the eastern sector, whereas systematic survey recorded 39 on-effort sightings there (@fig-obis). Because opportunistic recording tracks vessel routes and observer access rather than the underlying distribution of animals, the existing record systematically under-represents the eastern sector, a gap the present survey fills. Distribution inferences for the BGTW drawn from opportunistic data alone are therefore liable to spatial bias, and the systematic approach applied here, despite its own constraints, recovers occurrence in areas the opportunistic record misses. This supports the value of dedicated systematic monitoring as a complement to, rather than a replacement for, ongoing opportunistic and citizen-science recording [@bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001; @dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008].

## Conservation implications
During the sampling period, multiple incidents were observed in which recreational boat operators failed to comply with the Cetacean Protocol set out in the Gibraltar Marine Protection Regulations [-@zotero-item-2954]. On several occasions, fin whales (*Balaenoptera physalus*) were closely approached by recreational vessels during survey watches. Although the Department of Environment vessel was frequently present in the vicinity, no intervention with, or corrective engagement of, the offending operators was observed.

Vessel presence and close approaches are known to disrupt cetacean behaviour, eliciting shifts in activity budgets, group cohesion, and surfacing patterns [@hawkinsInteractiveBehavioursBottlenose2009; @neumannBehaviourEcologyCommon]. Studies in this area have recorded boat strikes and evasive behaviour in dolphins [@olaya-ponzoneVesselInducedBehavioural2025]; the presence of fin whales—a large, slow-surfacing species—within a major traffic separation scheme additionally raises a ship-strike risk that fixed protocol compliance alone may not mitigate. The repeated non-compliance observed here, combined with the apparent absence of on-water enforcement, indicates that the existing regulatory framework is not, in isolation, sufficient to safeguard cetaceans within the BGTW. This points to an implementation gap rather than a legislative one: adequate provisions exist under the Marine Protection Regulations [-@zotero-item-2954], but their protective value is contingent on consistent enforcement and operator awareness. Targeted outreach and operator education, alongside a visible enforcement presence at known hotspots (e.g. the southern tip of the peninsula, see @fig-kde_map), are therefore recommended, particularly during peak season, when vessel traffic in this area is greatest.

## Limitations
### Survey design and coverage
As outlined in the [Methods](#methods-sec), the survey design had to be revised from vessel-based line transects covering the entire `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst} to land-based observations covering only part of the study area. This change was necessitated by logistical constraints beyond the researcher's control: the intended survey vessel could not be launched, as arrangements for its transport and craning into the marina were not completed within the field-season window. Despite repeated assurances that the vessel would be made available, it did not enter the water, and, in consultation with the project supervisor, continued waiting was judged unfeasible given the fixed timeframe. Because the survey and sampling schedule had already been planned and proposed around vessel-based sampling, this represented one of the most significant project risks to be realised. The survey was consequently redesigned to accommodate these constraints.
As a result, the land-based stations sampled only parts of the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst}, and the density and distribution estimates should therefore be regarded as representative of the surveyed sectors rather than of the territory as a whole.


### Detection function and density estimation
Detection-function estimation was constrained by sample size. Reliable estimation typically requires 60–80 detections for line transects and more where covariates or complex key functions are fitted [@bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001; @bucklandDistanceSamplingMethods2015]. Per-station detection functions could not be reliably estimated at this sample size; the best-sampled station yielded only 54 detections; so a single pooled detection function was fitted across all stations, with observation height retained as a covariate to accommodate between-station differences in detectability. Pooling recovered an adequate sample (n = 98 at the selected 8,000 m truncation) and the resulting density estimates were robust to truncation choice, varying by less than 9% across the tested range and remaining well within their own confidence intervals (@tbl-sens). Nonetheless, the pooled approach assumes a common detection-function shape across stations, scaled by height; where the true shape differs between vantage points—plausible given their differing elevations and sight lines, this assumption introduces residual uncertainty not captured by the reported confidence intervals.

A further caveat of this approach was already expressed in previous studies [@keenDeterminingMarineMammal2021; @thomasDistanceSoftwareDesign2010; @howeDistanceSamplingCamera2017] concerns the assumptions of a fixed observation station. Because each station repeatedly scanned the same fixed sector rather than sampling randomly placed transects, animals cannot be assumed to be distributed independently of distance from the observer; moreover, detectability, modelled as a function of distance alone, may in practice have varied across the sector with bearing-dependent conditions such as glare and sea state. Neither assumption could be verified within the constrained timeframe, and both warrant explicit attention in future station-based surveys.


### Spatial representation
As implemented here, `#gls("kde")`{=typst} yields an aggregate, area-wide description of relative cetacean density. Because all on-effort sightings are pooled and weighted only by group size, the resulting surface reflects the combined space use of every detected animal and cannot resolve heterogeneity in habitat use among social groups or individuals. Distinct groups that occupy the same broad area while maintaining non-overlapping core areas are therefore indistinguishable within the pooled utilisation surface, and any fine-scale spatial partitioning is averaged out [@fiebergQuantifyingHomeRangeOverlap2005]. In the absence of individual- or group-level identification, for example through photo-identification or biotelemetry, the KDE surface characterises overall occurrence and areas of elevated density rather than group- or individual-level habitat utilisation.
Furthermore non-parametric estimators that construct the utilisation distribution from local geometry, such as the `#gls("locoh")`{=typst} method [@getzLoCoHNonparametericKernel2007], could refine the spatial representation of cetacean occurrence. Unlike the fixed-kernel approach applied here, `#gls("locoh")`{=typst} builds the distribution from local convex hulls of neighbouring sightings and therefore respects hard boundaries and interior exclusions—such as the coastline and the landmasses within the study area—rather than smoothing density across areas the animals cannot occupy.


### Unmodelled environmental drivers
Beyond the variables examined here, a range of environmental factors—including water depth, slope, sea surface temperature, and current speed—are known to influence cetacean occurrence and distribution [@gnoneCetaceansMediterraneanSea2023; @azzellinoCetaceanResponseEnvironmental2017]. These predictors were not incorporated into the present analyses and may explain additional variation in the observed spatial patterns. A more detailed investigation integrating the systematic survey data with environmental covariates is presented in a parallel study **[Hirst, unpublished]**, providing further insight into the environmental drivers of cetacean distribution within the study area.

Detection conditions varied in ways the final model only partially captures. The complexity of the Gibraltar coastline and the funnel effect of the Strait cause sea state to vary within a single sector; although sea state was assessed before every watch, locally elevated conditions may have caused missed detections even when overall conditions were suitable [@giacomaAreLandBased2013; @keenDeterminingMarineMammal2020]. Sea state, glare, wind speed, and wave height were each evaluated as candidate covariates during detection-function selection, but none improved model fit sufficiently to be retained over observation height (@tbl-model_summary). Their exclusion from the final model does not imply they had no effect on detectability, Beaufort sea state in particular is known to strongly influence cetacean detection [@barlowEvaluatingBiasLinetransect2026], but rather that the available sample size was insufficient to resolve their influence, a limitation directly attributable to the constrained field season. These conditions are likewise not reflected in the effort-uncorrected kernel density surface, which incorporates no detectability adjustment of any kind.

The reticle method itself introduces distance error. Although well established for shore-based distance estimation [@lerczakCalculatingSightingDistances1998], reticle graticules resolve angles only in fixed increments, discretising distance; observers interpolated to the nearest quarter-reticle to reduce this coarseness. Because distance is an approximately reciprocal function of the depression angle, a single reticle interval subtends a progressively larger distance band with range, spanning several kilometres at the far edge of the sampled area. This effect is most pronounced at low observer heights and was therefore greatest at Europa (18 m a.s.l.), although in practice most sightings there were recorded relatively close to shore, limiting its influence.

Finally, land-based distance sampling has rarely been adapted to small cetaceans, whose small size and brief, inconspicuous surfacing make them harder to detect at range than larger species. Where it has been applied to small cetaceans [@giacomaAreLandBased2013; @emblingHowMuchEffort2015; @keenDeterminingMarineMammal2020], surveys have typically been supplemented with standard or infrared cameras to improve detection. Successful land-based studies of small cetaceans nonetheless exist, though these have predominantly targeted larger-bodied Delphinidae such as *Tursiops truncatus* [@gutierrez-munozPatternsTrendsCetacean2021].


# SWOT Analysis

```{=typst}
#[
    #show table.cell: set text(size: 12pt)
    #show table.cell.where(y: 0): set text(size: 16pt, weight: "bold")
    #table(
    columns: (1fr, 1fr),
    stroke: none,
    align: (x, y) => if y == 0 { center } else { left + top },
    table.vline(x: 1, stroke: 0.5pt),
    table.header(
        table.cell(inset: (bottom: 25pt))[#smallcaps()[Strengths]],
        [#smallcaps[Weaknesses]],
    ),
    [
        - Land-based distance sampling provides a cost-effective alternative to vessel-based surveys by eliminating the need for boats, fuel, and associated operational costs.
        - The use of standardised 10-minute observation watches, interspersed with 20-minute off-effort periods, reduces observer fatigue while maintaining a consistent survey protocol.
        - Land-based surveys require relatively simple equipment, such as binoculars, making the methodology easy to implement, replicate, and adapt to a range of study sites and environmental conditions.
        - The survey protocol is straightforward to learn and can be readily taught to new observers, facilitating standardised data collection across personnel. 
        - The non-invasive nature of land-based surveys eliminates the risk of disturbing cetaceans through vessel presence, thereby reducing the potential for survey-induced changes in animal behaviour.
        - Fixed observation points enable repeated surveys of the same area over time, providing a consistent framework for monitoring temporal changes in cetacean distribution, abundance, and habitat use.
    ],
    [
        - Land-based surveys are time-intensive, requiring prolonged observation periods to obtain sufficient sample sizes and robust abundance estimates, particularly for species with low encounter rates.
        - Survey effort is highly dependent on favourable weather and sea conditions. Factors such as high sea state, glare, precipitation, and reduced visibility can substantially decrease detectability and may prevent surveys from being conducted.
        - The methodology relies on the visual detectability of cetaceans and is therefore biased towards larger species, larger group sizes, or individuals displaying conspicuous surface behaviour. Small groups, solitary individuals, or species with inconspicuous surfacing behaviour are more likely to remain undetected, potentially leading to underestimation of abundance.
        - The fixed nature of land-based observation points restricts survey coverage to the visible survey area, leaving offshore regions and areas obstructed by topography unsampled.
    ],
    )
]

```
---

```{=typst}
#[
    #show table.cell: set text(size: 12pt)
    #show table.cell.where(y: 0): set text(size: 16pt, weight: "bold")
    #table(
    columns: (1fr, 1fr),
    stroke: none,
    align: (x, y) => if y == 0 { center } else { left + top },
    table.vline(x: 1, stroke: 0.5pt),
    table.header(
        table.cell(inset: (bottom: 25pt))[#smallcaps()[Opportunities]],
        [#smallcaps[Threats]],
    ),
    [
        - Establishes a repeatable baseline enabling long-term monitoring of an IMMA currently lacking systematic data.
        - The low-cost, low-equipment protocol is well suited to adoption by the Department of Environment, NGOs, or citizen-science programmes, extending survey effort beyond a single field season.
        - Integration with parallel opportunistic, photo-identification, and environmental-covariate datasets could substantially strengthen distribution and abundance inference.
        - Growing policy attention to cetacean protection in the region (ACCOBAMS, IMMA designation) creates a receptive context for systematic monitoring data.
    ],
    [
        - Increasing vessel traffic and bunkering activity in the BGTW may alter cetacean distribution faster than infrequent surveys can detect.
        - Reliance on volunteer or student observers risks inconsistent long-term effort and protocol drift between personnel.
        - Reliance on external equipment provision and inter-institutional coordination, which, if unreliable, may constrain future survey scope and continuity.
        - Climate-driven shifts in prey distribution may decouple the observed baseline from future conditions, complicating change detection.
        - Political and jurisdictional complexity around Gibraltar's waters may constrain survey access or coordinated management action.
    ],
    )
]

```

# Conclusion
This study provides the first systematic, effort-corrected assessment of cetacean distribution and relative density within the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst}. Occurrence was spatially structured, concentrating at the southern tip of the peninsula, and encounter rates revealed an eastern sector that is both ecologically notable and absent from the existing opportunistic record. While the land-based design carries constraints; partial spatial coverage, sample-size limits, and detectability biases toward larger, conspicuous animals; it delivers a reproducible, low-cost framework that opportunistic recording alone cannot provide. The resulting estimates establish a provisional baseline for a data-poor `#gls("imma")`{=typst} under significant and increasing anthropogenic pressure. Continued systematic survey, ideally across multiple seasons and integrated with photo-identification and environmental data, is strongly recommended to determine whether the spatial patterns observed here persist and to support spatially targeted management within the territory.
